sections.h2: What is a Medicare Supplement (Medigap) plan?
sections.body: A Medicare Supplement plan is private insurance that pays your share of costs under Original Medicare (Part A and Part B), such as the 20% coinsurance Medicare doesn't cover, hospital deductibles, and coinsurance. Medigap works alongside Original Medicare, not in place of it. Because plans are federally standardized, a Plan G from one insurer covers exactly the same benefits as a Plan G from another, what changes is the monthly premium and the company's service. Medigap lets you see any doctor or hospital in the U.S. that accepts Medicare, with no networks and no referrals. To compare plans available where you live, call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).

sections.h2: What are the 10 standardized Medigap plans?
sections.body: Medigap is sold in 10 standardized plans, lettered A, B, C, D, F, G, K, L, M, and N. Each letter offers a fixed set of benefits set by federal law, so the only differences between insurers are price and reputation. Plans F and G offer the most comprehensive coverage, with Plan G being the top choice for people new to Medicare (Plan F is closed to those who became eligible on or after January 1, 2020). Plan N offers strong coverage at a lower premium with small copays. High-deductible versions of Plan G and Plan F trade lower premiums for a higher annual deductible. Massachusetts, Minnesota, and Wisconsin standardize their plans differently.

sections.h2: How much do Medicare Supplement plans cost in 2026?
sections.body: Medigap premiums vary widely by plan letter, your age, ZIP code, gender, tobacco use, and the insurer, typically ranging from roughly $90 to $300+ per month for Plan G. Two people with identical coverage can pay very different amounts depending on the carrier, which is why comparison shopping matters. Premiums also rise over time based on how the plan is rated: community-rated, issue-age-rated, or attained-age-rated. Beyond premium, your only Plan G cost is the annual Part B deductible ($257 in 2025; the 2026 figure is set by CMS each fall). A licensed agent at 1-800-MEDIGAP can compare every carrier in your area at no cost so you don't overpay for identical benefits.

sections.h2: When can you enroll in Medigap?
sections.body: The best time to buy is your Medigap Open Enrollment Period, the six months that begin the first month you're 65 or older AND enrolled in Part B. During this window you have guaranteed issue rights: insurers must sell you any plan they offer, at their best rate, regardless of health. Miss it and you may face medical underwriting, where carriers can charge more or decline you for pre-existing conditions, except in limited guaranteed-issue situations. There's no annual Medigap enrollment season like Medicare Advantage, you can apply any time, but approval outside your open enrollment isn't guaranteed. Don't risk losing your one-time guaranteed window; call 1-800-MEDIGAP to confirm your dates.

sections.h2: Medigap vs. Medicare Advantage: which is right for you?
sections.body: Medigap and Medicare Advantage are different ways to get Medicare coverage, and you can't use both at once. Medigap pairs with Original Medicare and a standalone Part D drug plan, offering nationwide doctor access, no networks, and predictable costs, but a monthly premium. Medicare Advantage (Part C) often has $0 premiums and bundles extras like dental and vision, but uses networks, requires referrals or prior authorizations, and charges copays as you use care. Frequent travelers, people who want freedom to choose specialists, and those who prefer budgeting a fixed premium often favor Medigap. The right answer depends on your health, budget, and providers, our agents explain the trade-offs free at 1-800-MEDIGAP.

faqs.q: Do Medicare Supplement plans cover prescription drugs?
faqs.a: No. Modern Medigap plans do not include prescription drug coverage. To cover medications, you'll need a separate standalone Medicare Part D drug plan, which you can enroll in alongside any Medigap policy. Bundling both gives you medical and drug coverage. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to set up both.

faqs.q: Can I keep my own doctor with a Medigap plan?
faqs.a: Yes. Medigap plans have no networks. You can see any doctor, specialist, or hospital in the United States that accepts Original Medicare, with no referrals required. This nationwide freedom is one of the biggest advantages of Medicare Supplement coverage over Medicare Advantage.

faqs.q: Is Plan F still available in 2026?
faqs.a: Plan F is still available, but only to people who became eligible for Medicare before January 1, 2020. If you turned 65 or qualified on or after that date, you cannot buy Plan F or high-deductible Plan F. Most newly eligible enrollees choose Plan G instead, which offers nearly identical coverage.

faqs.q: Which Medicare Supplement plan is the most popular?
faqs.a: Plan G is the most popular Medicare Supplement plan for people new to Medicare in 2026. It covers nearly everything Original Medicare leaves behind except the annual Part B deductible. Plan N and high-deductible Plan G are also popular among those seeking lower premiums.

faqs.q: Are Medigap plans standardized across insurers?
faqs.a: Yes. In most states, the federal government standardizes Medigap benefits, so a Plan G is identical no matter which insurer sells it. Only premium, customer service, and discounts differ. This means you can shop purely on price and company reputation. Massachusetts, Minnesota, and Wisconsin use different standardization systems.

faqs.q: Can I be denied a Medicare Supplement plan?
faqs.a: Outside your six-month Medigap Open Enrollment Period or a guaranteed-issue situation, insurers can use medical underwriting to charge more or deny coverage for pre-existing conditions. During your open enrollment window, you have guaranteed issue rights and cannot be turned down. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to check your eligibility.

faqs.q: Does Medigap cover me when I travel?
faqs.a: Yes. Because Medigap follows Original Medicare, you're covered by any provider nationwide that accepts Medicare. Several plans, including G and N, also cover 80% of emergency care during foreign travel, up to plan limits, after a deductible. This makes Medigap popular with snowbirds and travelers.

faqs.q: How do I get free help choosing a Medigap plan?
faqs.a: Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) to speak with a licensed insurance agent at no cost. They compare every standardized plan and carrier available in your ZIP code, explain the differences, and help you enroll, with no obligation and no pressure to buy.

sections.h2: How does Medicare Supplement insurance work?
sections.body: Medicare Supplement insurance pays after Original Medicare. When you receive care, Medicare pays its share first, then your Medigap policy automatically covers some or all of the remaining costs, depending on your plan. Because benefits are federally standardized, you always know exactly what's covered. There are no networks, you can see any provider in the U.S. that accepts Medicare. You pay a monthly premium to the insurer in addition to your Part B premium. This trade, a predictable premium for far fewer surprise bills, is why millions of seniors choose Medigap. To find your best rate, call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).

sections.h2: Who should buy Medicare Supplement insurance?
sections.body: Medicare Supplement insurance suits people who want predictable costs, the freedom to see any Medicare provider, and coverage that travels with them nationwide. It's especially valuable for those with ongoing health conditions, frequent travelers, snowbirds, and anyone who dislikes the prior-authorization and network rules of Medicare Advantage. The best time to enroll is during your six-month Medigap Open Enrollment Period, when you can't be turned down for health reasons. If you're approaching 65 or just signed up for Part B, call 1-800-MEDIGAP to confirm your enrollment window before it closes.

sections.h2: What does Medicare Supplement insurance not cover?
sections.body: Medicare Supplement insurance doesn't cover prescription drugs, you need a separate Part D plan for that. It also doesn't cover routine dental, vision, hearing aids, long-term custodial care, or cosmetic services. Medigap is designed to fill the gaps in Original Medicare's hospital and medical coverage, not to add new benefits. For drug coverage and extras, our agents can pair your Medigap plan with a Part D plan and other options. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for a complete coverage review.

faqs.q: Is Medicare Supplement insurance the same as Medigap?
faqs.a: Yes. Medicare Supplement insurance and Medigap are two names for the same product, private insurance that pays the out-of-pocket costs Original Medicare leaves behind. The term Medigap comes from the way these plans fill the gaps in Original Medicare coverage.

faqs.q: Do I still need Part B with Medicare Supplement insurance?
faqs.a: Yes. Medicare Supplement insurance only works alongside Original Medicare, so you must be enrolled in both Part A and Part B. You'll continue paying your Part B premium in addition to your Medigap premium. Medigap then covers your share of Medicare-approved costs.

faqs.q: Can my Medicare Supplement insurance be cancelled?
faqs.a: As long as you pay your premiums, your Medigap policy is guaranteed renewable, meaning the insurer cannot cancel it because of your health or claims. Policies can only be cancelled for non-payment or material misrepresentation on your application. This makes Medigap a stable, long-term choice.

faqs.q: How much does Medicare Supplement insurance cost?
faqs.a: Premiums vary by plan letter, age, location, gender, tobacco use, and insurer, often ranging from about $90 to $300+ per month for Plan G. Because benefits are standardized, the same coverage can cost very different amounts. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to compare carriers and avoid overpaying.

faqs.q: When should I buy Medicare Supplement insurance?

sections.h2: What's the core difference?
sections.body: Medigap works with Original Medicare: the government pays your claims, and your Medigap policy covers the leftover costs. You can see any provider nationwide that accepts Medicare, with no networks or referrals, and you add a standalone Part D plan for drugs. Medicare Advantage replaces Original Medicare with a private plan that bundles your coverage, often including drugs and extras like dental and vision, but restricts you to a network and may require prior authorization. The trade-off is freedom and predictability (Medigap) versus low upfront premiums and bundled extras (Advantage). Our agents explain both sides honestly, call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).

sections.h2: How do the costs compare?
sections.body: Medigap charges a monthly premium (often $90 to $300+ for Plan G) on top of your Part B premium, but then leaves you with minimal out-of-pocket costs when you receive care. Medicare Advantage often advertises $0 premiums, but you pay copays and coinsurance as you use services, up to an annual out-of-pocket maximum that can reach several thousand dollars. So Medigap front-loads cost into a predictable premium, while Advantage shifts cost to when you're sick. Which is cheaper overall depends on how much care you use. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to model both scenarios for your situation.

sections.h2: Which should you choose?
sections.body: Choose Medigap if you value seeing any doctor nationwide, travel often, have chronic conditions, or want predictable budgeting without prior-authorization hurdles. Choose Medicare Advantage if you want the lowest possible premium, like bundled extras such as dental and vision, and are comfortable using a network. Importantly, switching from Advantage to Medigap later may require medical underwriting, so your initial choice matters. A licensed agent at 1-800-MEDIGAP can weigh your doctors, medications, travel habits, and budget to recommend the right path, at no cost.

faqs.q: Can I have both Medigap and Medicare Advantage?
faqs.a: No. You cannot use a Medigap policy with a Medicare Advantage plan, and it's illegal for anyone to sell you a Medigap policy if they know you have Advantage. You must choose one path. Medigap works only with Original Medicare.

faqs.q: Is Medigap or Medicare Advantage cheaper?
faqs.a: Medicare Advantage usually has lower or $0 monthly premiums, while Medigap has higher premiums but lower costs when you need care. Which is cheaper overall depends on how much healthcare you use. Heavy healthcare users often spend less with Medigap. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to compare.

faqs.q: Can I switch from Medicare Advantage to Medigap?
faqs.a: Yes, but outside guaranteed-issue situations you may face medical underwriting and could be charged more or denied for health reasons. The best time to choose Medigap is during your initial open enrollment. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to review your switching options and timing.

faqs.q: Does Medigap or Medicare Advantage cover dental and vision?
faqs.a: Medicare Advantage often bundles dental, vision, and hearing extras; Medigap does not. Medigap focuses on covering Original Medicare's gaps. If you want those extras with Medigap, you'd add a separate dental or vision plan. Our agents can arrange that, call 1-800-MEDIGAP.

faqs.q: Which plan lets me keep my doctor?
faqs.a: Medigap lets you see any doctor or hospital nationwide that accepts Original Medicare, with no networks. Medicare Advantage restricts you to its network and may not include your current providers. If keeping your doctors matters most, Medigap is usually the safer choice.

sections.h2: When is your Medigap Open Enrollment Period?
sections.body: Your Medigap Open Enrollment Period automatically begins on the first day of the month you're both 65 or older and enrolled in Medicare Part B, and it lasts six months. This is a one-time, individual window, it's not the same as Medicare's Annual Enrollment Period in the fall. During these six months you have guaranteed issue rights: any insurer must sell you any Medigap plan it offers at its best available rate, regardless of your health history. Miss this window and you may have to answer health questions and could be charged more or declined. To confirm your exact dates, call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).

sections.h2: Why does this window matter so much?
sections.body: During your Medigap Open Enrollment Period, insurers cannot use medical underwriting, they can't deny you, charge you more, or impose waiting periods based on pre-existing conditions. Outside this window, unless you qualify for a limited guaranteed-issue situation, carriers can review your health and may reject your application or raise your premium. That makes these six months uniquely valuable, especially if you have any health conditions. It's the one time you're guaranteed access to every plan at the best price. Because the window doesn't reopen, acting before it closes is critical. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP now to lock in your coverage while you're guaranteed acceptance.

faqs.a: No. Medigap Open Enrollment is your personal six-month window tied to turning 65 and enrolling in Part B. Medicare's Annual Enrollment Period (October 15 to December 7) is for Medicare Advantage and Part D, not Medigap. They are completely separate, call 1-800-MEDIGAP to clarify.

faqs.q: Can I buy Medigap outside open enrollment?
faqs.a: Yes, you can apply any time, but outside your open enrollment or a guaranteed-issue situation, you may face medical underwriting and could be denied or charged more. Some states offer extra protections. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to see if you qualify for guaranteed acceptance.

faqs.q: Does my Medigap window reopen?
faqs.a: No. Your six-month Medigap Open Enrollment Period is a one-time window that doesn't reopen. That's why it's so important to act before it closes. Certain life events can trigger limited guaranteed-issue rights later, but they're narrow. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP before your window ends.

page.metaTitle: Switching Medigap Plans: How It Works | 1-800-MEDIGAP
page.metaDescription: Learn how to switch Medigap plans, when underwriting applies, and how to save. Free guidance from licensed agents. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.quickAnswer: You can switch Medigap plans any time of year, but outside guaranteed-issue situations, insurers can use medical underwriting and may charge more or deny you for health reasons. Switching makes sense when you find lower premiums for identical coverage or need different benefits, per Medicare.gov.
page.intro: You're not locked into your Medigap plan, you can switch, but timing and underwriting matter. Here's how to do it wisely.
page.heroSubhead: Switch smart, save money, avoid surprises. Get free switching guidance at 1-800-MEDIGAP.

sections.h2: Can you switch Medigap plans any time?
sections.body: Yes, there's no annual restriction on switching Medigap plans, you can apply to change plans or carriers at any time of year. The catch is medical underwriting. Unless you're in your open enrollment period or qualify for a guaranteed-issue situation, the new insurer can review your health and may charge more or decline you. If you're healthy, switching can save real money, since identical coverage often costs less from a different carrier. If you have health conditions, switching carries more risk. A licensed agent at 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) can check whether you'll likely pass underwriting before you apply, free.

sections.h2: When should you switch Medigap plans?
sections.body: Good reasons to switch include: your premium has risen sharply and another carrier offers the same coverage for less, your health needs have changed and a different plan letter fits better, or you want to drop unneeded benefits for a lower premium. Before switching, never cancel your current policy until the new one is approved and active, and watch for the 30-day free look period that lets you try the new plan risk-free. Some states have special rules (like birthday or anniversary rules) that ease switching. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to see if switching saves you money and whether you qualify for easier approval.

faqs.q: Can I switch Medigap plans without underwriting?
faqs.a: Only during your open enrollment period or a guaranteed-issue situation. Some states also offer birthday or anniversary rules that let you switch without underwriting. Otherwise, the new insurer can review your health. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to check if you qualify for guaranteed acceptance.

faqs.q: Is there a penalty for switching Medigap plans?
faqs.a: There's no government penalty for switching Medigap plans. The main risks are medical underwriting (you could be charged more or denied) and possibly a new waiting period for pre-existing conditions. If you pass underwriting, switching can save money. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to evaluate.

faqs.q: Will I have a gap in coverage if I switch?
faqs.a: You can avoid any gap by not cancelling your old policy until the new one is approved and active. Most plans also include a 30-day free look period to review the new policy risk-free. A licensed agent at 1-800-MEDIGAP can coordinate the timing for you.

faqs.q: Does switching reset my deductible?
faqs.a: Switching to a new Medigap plan can affect how deductibles apply, especially with high-deductible plans, since the new policy starts fresh. The Part B deductible is annual and tied to Medicare, not your Medigap plan. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to understand the specifics before switching.

faqs.q: Can I switch from Plan F to Plan G?
faqs.a: Yes, you can apply to switch from Plan F to Plan G to potentially lower your premium, but outside guaranteed-issue periods you may face underwriting. Many Plan F holders switch as Plan F premiums rise. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to compare and check your eligibility.

page.metaTitle: Is Plan F Still Available in 2026? | 1-800-MEDIGAP
page.metaDescription: Plan F is still available, but only if you were Medicare-eligible before 2020. See if you qualify. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.quickAnswer: Yes, Plan F is still available in 2026, but only to people who became eligible for Medicare before January 1, 2020. Those who qualified earlier can still enroll in or keep Plan F. Anyone newly eligible on or after that date cannot buy Plan F and typically chooses Plan G, per Medicare.gov.
page.intro: Plan F hasn't disappeared, but eligibility rules changed in 2020. Here's exactly who can still get it.
page.heroSubhead: Still available, with one key date. Check your eligibility at 1-800-MEDIGAP.

sections.h2: Who can still buy Plan F?
sections.body: Plan F is still sold, but only to people who were eligible for Medicare before January 1, 2020, meaning you turned 65 or otherwise qualified before that date. If that's you, you can still apply for Plan F or keep an existing policy. If you became eligible on or after January 1, 2020, federal law (MACRA) blocks you from buying Plan F because it covers the Part B deductible, which newer plans can't. Most newly eligible beneficiaries choose Plan G instead, which offers nearly identical coverage. Not sure which date applies to you? Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) and we'll confirm your eligibility in minutes.

sections.h2: Should you keep Plan F if you have it?
sections.body: If you already have Plan F, you can keep it for as long as you pay premiums, it's guaranteed renewable. But there's a consideration: because Plan F is closed to new enrollees, its risk pool is aging and shrinking, which can push premiums up faster over time. Some Plan F holders switch to Plan G or high-deductible Plan F to save money, though switching may require underwriting outside guaranteed-issue periods. Whether to stay or switch depends on your premium trend, health, and budget. A licensed agent at 1-800-MEDIGAP can compare your current rate against alternatives for free.

faqs.q: Why was Plan F discontinued for new enrollees?
faqs.a: Federal law (MACRA) barred Medigap plans from covering the Part B deductible for people newly eligible for Medicare on or after January 1, 2020. Because Plan F covers that deductible, it's closed to anyone who qualified after that date. Those eligible earlier can still get it.

faqs.q: Can I get Plan F if I turn 65 in 2026?
faqs.a: No. If you turn 65 in 2026, you became eligible for Medicare after January 1, 2020, so you cannot buy Plan F. Plan G offers nearly identical coverage and is the top choice for new enrollees. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to compare your options.

faqs.q: Is Plan G a good replacement for Plan F?
faqs.a: Yes. Plan G covers everything Plan F does except the annual Part B deductible, so it's the closest available alternative for new enrollees. Many people find Plan G's lower premium more than offsets paying the deductible. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to compare Plan G rates.

faqs.q: Will Plan F premiums keep rising?
faqs.a: Because Plan F is closed to new enrollees, its risk pool is aging, which can push premiums up faster than open plans over time. Some holders switch to Plan G to save. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to compare your Plan F premium against alternatives for free.

faqs.q: Can I switch back to Plan F later?
faqs.a: Only if you were eligible for Medicare before January 1, 2020. If you qualify, you can apply for Plan F, but outside guaranteed-issue periods you may face medical underwriting. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to check your eligibility and timing.

page.metaDescription: Medigap plans don't cover prescriptions, you need Part D. Learn how to pair them. Free help at 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.quickAnswer: No. Modern Medigap plans do not cover prescription drugs. To get medication coverage, you must enroll in a separate standalone Medicare Part D plan, which works alongside any Medigap policy. Older Medigap plans sold before 2006 may include limited drug coverage, but new plans do not, per Medicare.gov.
page.intro: Medigap covers medical gaps, not medications. Here's how to get prescription coverage alongside your Medigap plan.
page.heroSubhead: Medigap plus Part D equals complete coverage. Set up both at 1-800-MEDIGAP.

sections.h2: Why don't Medigap plans cover drugs?
sections.body: Medigap plans don't cover prescription drugs because, since 2006, Medicare separated drug coverage into its own program, Part D. Before that, some Medigap plans included limited drug benefits, but those plans are no longer sold, and any covering drugs were essentially grandfathered. Today, every standardized Medigap plan (A through N) covers only the gaps in Original Medicare's hospital and medical benefits, not medications. To cover your prescriptions, you enroll in a standalone Part D plan that pairs with your Medigap policy. This two-policy approach, Medigap plus Part D, gives you comprehensive medical and drug coverage. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) to set up both.

sections.h2: How do you get prescription coverage with Medigap?
sections.body: To cover medications alongside Medigap, you enroll in a standalone Medicare Part D prescription drug plan. You can do this when you first get Medicare or during the Annual Enrollment Period (October 15 to December 7) each year. Part D plans vary by the drugs they cover (their formulary), pharmacy networks, and costs, so it's important to match a plan to your specific medications. Choosing the wrong Part D plan can cost hundreds extra per year. Enrolling in Part D when first eligible also helps you avoid a late-enrollment penalty. A licensed agent at 1-800-MEDIGAP can match a Part D plan to your prescriptions, free.

faqs.q: Do any Medigap plans include drug coverage?
faqs.a: No new Medigap plans include drug coverage. Some plans sold before 2006 had limited drug benefits and were grandfathered, but they're no longer available to buy. For prescriptions today, you need a separate Part D plan. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to add drug coverage.

faqs.q: Can I have both Medigap and Part D?
faqs.a: Yes. Medigap and Part D are designed to work together, Medigap covers medical gaps while Part D covers prescriptions. You can enroll in both, and most people with Medigap also carry a standalone Part D plan. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to set up both.

faqs.q: What happens if I don't get Part D?
faqs.a: If you go without creditable drug coverage after becoming eligible, you may owe a Part D late-enrollment penalty that's added to your premium for life once you do enroll. Even if you take few medications, enrolling on time avoids this. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for guidance.

faqs.q: How do I choose a Part D plan?
faqs.a: Match the plan's formulary to your specific medications, then compare premiums, deductibles, and pharmacy networks. The wrong plan can cost hundreds extra per year. A licensed agent at 1-800-MEDIGAP can compare Part D plans against your exact prescription list, free.

page.metaDescription: Learn what Medigap underwriting questions cover and when they apply. Get help passing underwriting. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.quickAnswer: Medigap underwriting questions are health questions insurers ask when you apply outside your open enrollment or a guaranteed-issue period. They cover conditions, medications, and recent treatments, and can lead to higher premiums or denial. During your open enrollment, no underwriting applies, per Medicare.gov.
page.intro: Outside your protected windows, applying for Medigap means answering health questions. Here's what underwriting involves and how to navigate it.
page.heroSubhead: Know the questions before you apply. Get expert underwriting help at 1-800-MEDIGAP.

sections.h2: What are Medigap underwriting questions?
sections.body: Medigap underwriting questions are health-related questions an insurer asks to assess your risk when you apply outside a protected enrollment window. They typically ask about current and recent medical conditions, prescription medications, hospitalizations, surgeries, and pending treatments. Based on your answers, and sometimes a prescription or medical record check, the insurer decides whether to approve you, charge a higher rate, or decline coverage. Underwriting only applies outside your six-month Medigap Open Enrollment Period or a guaranteed-issue situation. Because different carriers weigh conditions differently, one may decline you while another approves you. A licensed agent at 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) knows which carriers are most lenient for your situation.

sections.h2: How can you improve your chances of approval?
sections.body: The surest way to avoid underwriting entirely is to apply during your Medigap Open Enrollment Period or a guaranteed-issue situation, when health questions don't apply. If you must answer underwriting questions, accuracy matters, misrepresenting your health can void your policy later. Because each insurer has different underwriting guidelines, the same health profile can be approved by one carrier and declined by another. Working with an experienced agent helps you apply to the carrier most likely to accept you, avoiding wasted applications and potential declines on your record. Before you apply anywhere, call 1-800-MEDIGAP for a free review of which carriers best fit your health history.

faqs.q: When do Medigap underwriting questions apply?
faqs.a: Underwriting questions apply when you buy Medigap outside your six-month open enrollment period or a guaranteed-issue situation. During those protected windows, no health questions are asked and you can't be denied. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to confirm whether underwriting applies to you.

faqs.q: Can I be denied Medigap because of underwriting?
faqs.a: Yes, outside protected windows, insurers can decline you or charge more based on your answers to underwriting questions. But because carriers have different guidelines, one may approve you when another declines. A licensed agent at 1-800-MEDIGAP can match you to the most lenient carrier.

faqs.q: What conditions affect Medigap underwriting?
faqs.a: Insurers commonly ask about recent hospitalizations, ongoing conditions like diabetes or heart disease, certain medications, and pending surgeries or treatments. Each carrier weighs conditions differently. A licensed agent at 1-800-MEDIGAP knows which insurers are most likely to approve your specific health profile.

faqs.q: Should I answer underwriting questions honestly?
faqs.a: Always. Misrepresenting your health on a Medigap application can lead the insurer to void your policy or deny claims later. Honesty protects your coverage. An agent at 1-800-MEDIGAP can help you apply to carriers most likely to accept your accurate health history.

faqs.q: How do I avoid Medigap underwriting?
faqs.a: Apply during your six-month Medigap Open Enrollment Period or a guaranteed-issue situation, when no health questions are asked. If your window has passed, some states offer extra protections. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to see if you qualify for guaranteed acceptance and skip underwriting.

page.intro: Plan G's price isn't one number, it depends on several personal factors. Here's what drives Plan G cost in 2026 and how to pay less.
page.heroSubhead: Same Plan G, very different prices. Find your lowest 2026 rate at 1-800-MEDIGAP.

sections.h2: What does Plan G cost in 2026?
sections.body: Plan G premiums in 2026 commonly range from roughly $90 to $300+ per month, but your actual price depends on your age, ZIP code, gender, tobacco use, and the insurer you choose. Beyond premium, your only expected out-of-pocket cost under Plan G is the annual Part B deductible ($257 in 2025; the 2026 amount is set by CMS each fall), after which Plan G covers nearly all Medicare-approved costs. Because every insurer's Plan G provides identical benefits, the same coverage can cost hundreds of dollars more per year from one carrier than another. Comparing carriers is the key to a lower price. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for free quotes.

sections.h2: What factors affect your Plan G premium?
sections.body: Several factors shape your Plan G premium: your age (older applicants generally pay more), ZIP code (local healthcare costs and competition vary), gender, tobacco use, and the insurer's rating method. There are three rating types, community-rated (same price regardless of age), issue-age-rated (based on your age at purchase, doesn't rise with age), and attained-age-rated (starts lower but increases as you age). The attained-age plan that's cheapest today may not stay cheapest, so understanding the rating method matters for long-term cost. A licensed agent at 1-800-MEDIGAP can compare carriers and rating methods to find your best long-term value, not just the lowest first-year price.

faqs.q: How much is Plan G per month in 2026?
faqs.a: Plan G premiums in 2026 typically range from about $90 to $300+ per month, depending on your age, ZIP code, gender, tobacco use, and insurer. Identical coverage varies widely by carrier. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for free quotes from every insurer in your area.

faqs.q: Why does Plan G cost different amounts?
faqs.a: Because Plan G benefits are federally standardized, insurers compete only on price. Each carrier sets its own premium and rating method, so the same coverage can cost hundreds more from one company than another. Comparing carriers is how you save, call 1-800-MEDIGAP.

faqs.q: What is the Part B deductible for Plan G in 2026?
faqs.a: Plan G requires you to pay the annual Part B deductible (it was $257 in 2025; CMS sets the 2026 figure each fall). After meeting it, Plan G covers nearly all your Medicare-approved costs. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for the current 2026 deductible and rates.

faqs.q: Will my Plan G premium increase each year?
faqs.a: It depends on the rating method. Attained-age-rated plans rise as you age; issue-age and community-rated plans are more stable. All plans can adjust for inflation and claims. Reviewing your rate yearly helps you save. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for a free annual comparison.

faqs.q: How can I lower my Plan G cost in 2026?
faqs.a: Compare carriers (identical coverage varies in price), ask about tobacco-free and household discounts, and consider high-deductible Plan G for a lower premium. Reviewing rates annually also helps. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for free help finding your lowest Plan G price.
